ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper presents a notable and surprising perspective: that single-layer CNNs with random, multi-scale filters can synthesize natural textures that often rival the VGG-based multi-layer, pretrained approach (see "Our main contributions" and Secs. 6–7). It systematically compares many filter types/sizes (random, Fourier, k-means, PCA, multi-scale) and introduces a quantitative VGG-loss evaluation, providing substantive empirical evidence that depth and supervised training are not necessary for strong texture synthesis. This challenges prevailing assumptions from Gatys et al. (2015) and earlier reports, so it is more than an incremental tweak, but it does build on the existing Gram-matrix framework rather than proposing an entirely new algorithmic paradigm—hence not the topmost novelty score.

CLARITY.justification: Overall the paper is organized into a conventional and logical structure (Introduction → model → synthesis → evaluation → results → discussion) and clearly lists its main contributions early in the Introduction. The mathematical notation for the Gram matrices and losses is mostly consistent and the key equations (1)–(7) are defined, which aids reproducibility. However, presentation quality is degraded by duplicated/repeated blocks of text (multiple repeated “1 INTRODUCTION” sections and verbatim paragraphs in the Appendix), several lexical errors (e.g. “then” vs. “than”, “inpaited”), and minor formatting issues that make some passages hard to follow; figure references (e.g. Fig. 1, Fig. 4) are frequent and seem informative but the manuscript’s repetition and small grammatical problems reduce readability.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper's main claim — that single-layer CNNs with random filters can synthesize high-quality textures — is clearly stated (Intro) and supported by extensive empirical comparisons across many architectures and filter types (Secs. 2–6). The mathematical definitions and objectives (Gram matrix (Eq.1), normalized distances (Eqs.2–3) and loss/optimization (Eqs.4–7)) are correct and complete for the proposed method. However, the evaluation relies primarily on a VGG-based loss as a proxy for human perception (Sec.5) and qualitative examples rather than psychophysical studies or statistical tests; experimental selection/variation reporting is limited (Secs.6, App.), which leaves room for selection bias and prevents stronger quantitative claims. Overall the technical treatment is sound but the empirical evaluation lacks rigorous human/ statistical validation, so conclusions should be taken as well-supported for textures under the tested conditions but not universally proven.
